comments.text: If you can get a computer to calculate the largest size of an admissible subset $A$ of $\{1,\dots,N\}$ for all small $N$ (e.g., $N \leq 20$) then you can consider submitting it to the OEIS. (Presumably with SAT solvers one can go much higher than this, but I am not very familiar with these tools. But brute force search should certainly reach $N=20$ at least. Or maybe there are off-the-shelf graph theory tools to find largest cliques etc. that could apply here?)

Checked OEIS but no hit (for the first question, it starts with [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 10, 11, 12, 12, 13, 14, 14, 15, 15, 16, 17, 18, 18, 19, 20, 21, 21, 22, 23, 24, 25, 26, 27, 27, 28]) The example was mostly there as people might see some pattern/idea there.
Stijn, please submit a new sequence to OEIS with your calculations! You can cite this problem and update the ongoing collaborative project to add such sequences.

Using a SAT solver, for the first part you can easily get beyond 1700 terms with runtimes of less than a minute. I've extended the OEIS page (A384927) with these elements. Interestingly, the density of these elements is roughly decreasing, but was still $> 0.71$ for all the values I computed. The minimum I've found occurred at $N = 1716$ with a subset of size $1222$. I tried computing for $N = 2000$, but this computation started taking a very long time so I stopped it early. For the second part, I was able to compute up to $N = 580$ and a subset of size $406$, and the minimum density found was $0.6996\dots$ with $N = 506$ and a subset of size $354$. ------ UPDATE: By switching to Google ORTools and using a MILP, I was able to push this a lot further to $N = 5000$ for the first part, and $N = 1000$ for the second part, and further computation is still possible although it will likely take 10s of minutes to compute for each value. The ratio has still stayed above $0.7$, with the lowest occurring at $N = 4991$ yielding a subset of size $3521$. For the second part, the ratio still stayed above $0.69$ - the lowest I found was at $N = 980$ with a subset of size $679$. I was hoping I would get something for the first part dropping below $0.7$, but it seems we need to compute much larger terms to get there.

For the second question, one can consider a greedy algorithm, i.e. consider the set $A$ where $a\in A$ if and only if $a+b \nmid 2ab$ for all $b<a$ with $b\in A$. At $N=500$, the greedy algorithm produces a subset of size $343$, for a density of $.688$, a small loss compared to the optimal $.7$, but after that the density drops much more quickly, attaining a set of size $3254$ and a density of $.6508$ when $N=5000$. (I used ChatGPT to do these calculations.) The greedy set includes all numbers $a$ such that $a+b \nmid 2ab$ for all $b<a$. These include the numbers with prime factorization $p_1 \times \dots \times p_k$ such that $p_1\times \dots \times p_{i-1} \leq \frac{p_i}{2}$ for all $i$. If I calculated right, the number of numbers $\leq N$ with such prime factorization is $N / \log N^{ .3588\dots +o(1)}$, where $.3588\dots$ is the unique solution of $2^{\alpha-1}= 1-\alpha$. So this gives a lower bound on $f_2(N)$, but one that may be far from the truth.

(This comment has been moved here from 1063.) We obtain $f_1(N) \leq (0.7769 + o(1))N$ and $f_2(N) \leq (0.7630 + o(1))N$. Both improve van Doorn's $25/28$ bound. Writeup at https://leon2k2k2k.github.io/erdos327.pdf The idea is a smooth--rough decomposition. Fix a finite set of primes $P$ and write each integer $n = m \cdot c$ with $c$ $P$-smooth and $m$ $P$-rough; this partitions any candidate set $A$ into fibers $A_m = \{c : mc \in A\}$, and the $k$-admissibility constraint passes through (each fiber is itself $k$-admissible). The global bound on $|A|$ then reduces to bounding $k$-admissible subsets of the smooth integers, weighted by the density of rough multipliers --- a finite quantity for any cutoff. The headline numbers above are computed at $P = \{2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13\}$ and cutoff at $2000$. Running the same machinery on the $a + b \mid 3ab$ variant gives $f_3(N) \leq (0.6089 + o(1))N$, hinting that $f_2(N) \geq (1/2 + o(1))N$. Found with the help of GPT-5.5 + Rethlas; verified by hand.

Congrats! Standard check found no issues. However, the certified computation was not included/linked to in the paper, making that part unverifiable. We also noted that the "hint" from $f_3$ bound to $f_2$ bound didn't seem to be very clear.

Thanks for the check! The certified computation script is now linked in the updated writeup: erdos327_cert.py. Run with python3 erdos327_cert.py 1 2000 for $k=1$ (gives $0.7769$) and python3 erdos327_cert.py 2 2000 for $k=2$ (gives $0.7630$). Requires PuLP. Regarding the $f_3$ "hint": the observation is purely empirical — $f_3(N)$ is consistently lower than for $f_2(N)$, suggesting that the $a+b \mid 3ab$ condition is more restrictive than $a+b \mid 2ab$. Just numerical evidence.

An observation, more general states for sets with no $a<b$ satisfying $a+b \mid k ab$, which seemed important at some point, but didn't do the job at the end: Let $P(x)$ denote the largest prime divisor of $x$. Let $A=\{1 \le x \le N \colon P(x) \ge (kN)^{1/2} \}$. Now if $a<b$ both belong to $A$, where $P(a)=p$ and $P(b)=q$ are the largest prime divisors. If $p \not =q$, then $a+b$ is relative prime with both $p$ and $q$ and thus $pq(a+b) \nmid kab$ since $pq(a+b)>kN (a+b)>kab.$ If $p=q$, the property repeats on $a/p,b/p$.

If $A$ satisfies the condition such that for $a,b\in A$ and $a
eq b$ then $a+b 
mid ab$, we say $A$ satisfies (1). If $B$ satisfies the condition such that for $a,b\in B$ and $a
eq b$ then $a+b 
mid 2ab$, we say $B$ satisfies (2). Then there exists an $A\subseteq\{1,\ldots,2N\}$ satisfying (1) and a constant $c>0$ such that $A$ contains $cN$ even numbers if and only if there exists a $B\subseteq\{1,\ldots,N\}$ satisfying (2) such that $|B|=cN$. So a positive answer to the first question implies a negative answer to the third question. Let $A\subseteq\{1,\ldots,2N\}$ satisfy (1) and let $B\subseteq A$ be the set of even numbers in $A$. Assume there exists $c>0$ such that $|B|=cN$. Then for any $2a,2b\in B$ where $2a
eq 2b$, we have $2a+2b
mid 4ab$. Equivalently, $a+b
mid 2ab$. Then letting $C=\{a\mid 2a\in B\}$, we see that $C$ satisfies (2), $C\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ and $|C|=cN$. Let $B\subseteq\{1,\ldots,N\}$ satisfy (2) such that $|B|=cN$. Then for $a,b\in B$, $a
eq b$, we have $a+b
mid 2ab$. Equivalently, $2a+2b
mid 4ab$. Let $A=\{2a\mid a\in B\}$. Then we have $A\subseteq\{1,\ldots, 2N\}$, $A$ satisfies (1) and $A$ contains $cN$ even numbers.
